title: The Fourth Sunday of Advent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
December 19, 2021 | Fourth Sunday of Advent

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Fourth Sunday of Advent, the final Sunday before Christmas Day, marking the climax of the Advent season in the Christian calendar. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date would have included 2 Samuel 7:1-11, 16 (the Davidic covenant), Romans 16:25-27, and Luke 1:26-38 (the Annunciation to Mary), focusing on promises fulfilled and divine preparation for Christ's coming. The liturgical color would have been deep purple or blue, and the fourth Advent candle would be lit alongside the previous three, with many churches lighting a Christ candle as well on this final Sunday before Christmas.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was preparing for its 80th General Convention, scheduled for July 2022 in Baltimore, with pre-convention discussions underway about church structure and theology. In the Diocese of Colorado, Bishop Gluvna Franklin-Cox (elected in 2019) was in her second year of leadership, overseeing diocesan recovery and growth following pandemic disruptions. No major controversies or significant diocesan news events were documented in the immediate week before this date, though the denomination continued navigating post-pandemic reopening protocols and vaccine discussions within congregations.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the midst of typical winter weather patterns for mid-December, with temperatures in the 30s-40s Fahrenheit and occasional snow possible, though major winter storms had not yet impacted the region. Colorado's COVID-19 case rates were elevated due to the emerging Omicron variant, which was becoming the dominant strain nationally and prompting increased concern about holiday gatherings. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, where St. John's Cathedral is located, was preparing for holiday festivities and local businesses were in peak holiday shopping season, though inflation and supply chain concerns were affecting consumer sentiment.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The United States was experiencing a major surge in COVID-19 cases driven by the highly transmissible Omicron variant, with hospitalizations rising nationwide and the CDC updating guidance on testing and isolation protocols just days before this date (CDC announced updated guidance on December 17). President Biden held a December 16 press conference addressing rising inflation and economic concerns as the nation headed into the final weeks of 2021. The Kyle Rittenhouse acquittal (November 19) was still reverberating through national political discourse, and Congress was negotiating year-end spending bills and debt ceiling extensions with Republican opposition creating legislative uncertainty.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Australian Open tennis tournament was in final preparation stages for its January 2022 opening, with significant controversy building around vaccination requirements for international players entering Australia. The Russian military buildup on the Ukrainian border was accelerating and becoming increasingly prominent in Western media, though major military action had not yet commenced; U.S. intelligence agencies were warning of imminent invasion possibilities. China was reporting zero COVID cases under its strict zero-COVID policy, which was affecting global supply chains and creating tension with international trading partners over border closures.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in mid-December 2021 existed in a state of anxious anticipation about holiday gatherings—eager for normalcy after two years of pandemic disruptions, but increasingly worried about the Omicron variant's potential to disrupt Christmas plans. The cultural zeitgeist blended Christmas preparation and festivity with underlying anxiety about disease transmission, inflation affecting holiday spending, and political polarization that had become particularly acute around pandemic response measures. Spider-Man: No Way Home had just been released on December 17 to massive box office success and cultural phenomenon status, providing one of the year's brightest entertainment moments; holiday movies and specials filled screens, offering escapism during an uncertain period.
